text.title: Senior Promotion Case (Outsource Shop) Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules that distinguish an outsource-shop promotion case from a generic STAR write-up. Skipping any rule weakens the case against the tri-axis rubric.

rule.statement: The case MUST cover all three rubric axes — Technical Contribution, Revenue/Account Impact, Utilization — with explicit headed sections. Missing any axis = the committee will mark "incomplete" regardless of strength elsewhere.
rule.rationale: Candidates over-index on the axis they're proud of (usually technical) and lose points on the others by silence. The committee scores per-axis; silence = zero.

rule.statement: Every claim MUST cite a numeric anchor: lines/PRs/design-docs, MRR/ARR/billable-revenue, billable utilization %. Adjective-only claims ("significantly improved", "led architecture") = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Outsource committees compare candidates head-to-head. Without numbers, the strongest narrative loses to the candidate with hard numbers in a weaker story.

rule.statement: Revenue/Account section MUST attribute revenue impact with a defensible mechanism: scope-expansion won, contract-renewal saved, billable-rate raise justified, account-NPS uplift, escalation salvaged. Hand-wave revenue claims without a mechanism = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Outsource firms make money on billable hours and account expansion. "I delivered features" is table stakes; promotion happens when the firm earns more because of you.

rule.statement: Utilization section MUST report billable % over the window (typically 6 or 12 months), bench days, and any pre-sales / internal-tools time. Single-quarter snapshots = REJECT — the case window MUST match the firm's review window.
rule.rationale: A cherry-picked quarter mis-represents sustained performance. Window-matching is what auditors call "comparable basis."

rule.statement: The case MUST include an "Objections & Responses" appendix that anticipates the 3 most likely committee objections (per axis) and pre-rebuts them with evidence. Cases without anti-rubric prep get blindsided in the live review.
rule.rationale: Outsource committees grill candidates on weaknesses; pre-rebutting flips the dynamic from defensive to confirmatory. This is the single highest-leverage section.

rule.statement: The case MUST be cosigned by the candidate's direct manager AND at least one delivery / account lead from a major project. Self-only cases score worse — committee weights co-sign as social proof.
rule.rationale: Manager-only co-sign reads as "boss is friendly"; adding an account lead from outside the candidate's reporting chain is the credibility multiplier.

example.title: Case section header
example.text: candidate: Olena V. window: 2025-11 to 2026-05 (6mo) axes: {Technical: senior architect on Account-X migration, 14 design docs, 0 P1 escalations | Revenue: scope expansion +$340k ARR, contract renewal saved on Account-Y | Utilization: 92% billable, 0 bench days, +20% pre-sales} cosigners: @manager-lead @account-lead-X objections_prep: 3 anticipated, all rebutted with evidence
